trajectory_summary: This is a structurally thin call that reaches a booking outcome without building any of the emotional scaffolding the schema requires. Bucky clears the mechanical qualifiers — address, homeowner status, roof type, credit — but skips the emotional anchor entirely (interest_in_solar never asked), has no bill to work with, and books on a customer who has articulated a purely conditional interest. The appointment exists on the calendar; the customer's investment in showing up does not.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked appointment, but the booking is structurally fragile. Deepak has no bill yet (just moved in this month), the interest_in_solar verify item was never asked, no emotional anchor was set, and Deepak's closing statement frames the appointment as a cost-evaluation exercise rather than a committed sit. The v1 tag of 'booked' is technically accurate but masks a high no-show risk. This appointment should be flagged as conditional.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: And what utility company are you with?
primary_coaching_focus.why: The interest_in_solar question is the schema's designated emotional anchor and the only verify item that is depth-graded. Bucky skipped it entirely, moving from title confirmation directly to utility company. Every downstream weakness on this call — the generic text, the conditional booking, the closer walking in blind — traces back to this single omission. This is the one coaching beat that, if fixed, changes the shape of every call Bucky runs.

key_moments.quote: Can can you hold for a moment? Can you I got it. Just give me one moment.
key_moments.stewart_read: Deepak is pulled into a side transaction for ~90 seconds at the top of the call. Bucky holds the line silently. Strategic hypothesis: holding is a deliberate choice to preserve the lead rather than lose it by hanging up. Reactive hypothesis: Bucky defaults to waiting because he has no protocol for this situation. The evidence is ambiguous — there is no verbal move from Bucky during the hold, and no offer-to-call-back is made. Coaching point stands regardless of intent: a brief verbal anchor ('Take your time — I'll be right here, and if it's easier I can call you back in five minutes') would have managed the dead air and given Deepak a graceful exit option that he could decline, strengthening his commitment to the call.

key_moments.quote: So it's still empty lot, but I know the home is built. It's a single family. Right?
key_moments.stewart_read: Bucky spends approximately two minutes (02:02–02:37) working through an address mapping problem — the satellite tool shows an empty lot for Deepak's address. On a call with an effective runtime of roughly 4.5 minutes of actual conversation, this is a disproportionate time burn. The address was ultimately confirmed verbally by Deepak; the mapping issue did not block qualification. Bucky could have confirmed the address verbally and moved on, deferring the mapping lookup to the closer or a back-office step.

Deepak discloses that his son is on the title. Per schema protocols.adult_child_decision_maker, the correct response is to include the son in the appointment. Bucky acknowledges with 'Okay.' and moves directly to utility company. The son is never mentioned again — the appointment is booked without confirming whether the son will be present.

key_moments.quote: And what utility company are you with?
key_moments.stewart_read: The interest_in_solar verify item — labeled in the schema as 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR' and depth-graded — is never asked on this call. Bucky moves from title/co-owners directly to utility company, skipping the one question that would have surfaced Deepak's motivation, bill pain, or emotional investment in solar. Without this anchor, the entire pitch has no emotional foundation, and the appointment is booked on procedural momentum alone.
